1:
"Then we turned, and took our journey into the wilderness by the way of the Red sea, as the LORD spake unto me: and we compassed mount Seir many days." The witnesses note the turning back into the wilderness, and the long compassing of Mount Seir. The witnesses note that Gill notes they turned from Kadesh, and so turned their backs on the land of Canaan, on whose borders they had been. The witnesses note that Henry marks the short account of a long stay — they compassed Mount Seir nearly thirty-eight years, wandering in the deserts of Seir. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the then we turned — back into the wilderness from Kadesh; the by the Red sea — the long way round; the compassed Mount Seir — nearly thirty-eight years; the the wasted years — unbelief lengthening the way.

The witnesses agree the brief words cover the weary length of the wilderness years: nearly thirty-eight years were spent compassing Mount Seir, the long detour of a generation under judgment, turning away from the very land they might have entered; the short account of so long a wandering is itself solemn, the wasted years passing in a sentence — a quiet reminder how unbelief lengthens the way and how long the consequences of it endure.

So the witnesses make the verse compassing Seir: The witnesses note the then we turned: back into the wilderness from Kadesh. The witnesses note the by the Red sea: the long way round. The witnesses note the compassed Mount Seir: nearly thirty-eight years. The witnesses note the the wasted years: unbelief lengthening the way. The expositors observe the then we turned: back into the wilderness from Kadesh. The expositors observe the by the Red sea: the long way round. The expositors observe the compassed Mount Seir: nearly thirty-eight years. The expositors observe the the wasted years: unbelief lengthening the way. The believer is taught to mark the then we turned, the by the Red sea, the compassed Mount Seir, the the wasted years, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel compassed Mount Seir many days — nearly thirty-eight years of wandering, the long detour of a generation under judgment, unbelief lengthening the way.
2:
"And the LORD spake unto me, saying," The witnesses note God speaking to Moses at Mount Seir, now to set them moving northward. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was while about Mount Seir. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the command to turn northward, round the southern end of Seir, without warring with Edom. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD spake — to Moses at Mount Seir; the to turn northward — ending the long delay; the toward their possession — the advance ordered; the the judgment turning — toward its end at last; the at God's word — his people moving and staying as he speaks.

The witnesses agree the word of the LORD now ended the long delay and ordered their advance: after the years of compassing Seir, God spoke to set them on their way at last, northward toward the lands they should possess; the renewing of the divine word marks the turning of the long judgment toward its end, the wandering generation now wasted and the new one to be led forward — God's speech the spring of every movement of his people, who go and stay at his word.

So the witnesses make the verse God speaks again: The witnesses note the the LORD spake: to Moses at Mount Seir. The witnesses note the to turn northward: ending the long delay. The witnesses note the toward their possession: the advance ordered. The witnesses note the the judgment turning: toward its end at last. The witnesses note the at God's word: his people moving and staying as he speaks. The expositors observe the the LORD spake: to Moses at Mount Seir. The expositors observe the to turn northward: ending the long delay. The expositors observe the toward their possession: the advance ordered. The expositors observe the the judgment turning: toward its end at last. The expositors observe the at God's word: his people moving and staying as he speaks. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD spake, the to turn northward, the toward their possession, the the judgment turning, the at God's word, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that the LORD spake to set Israel moving northward — his word ending the long delay, his people going and staying at his word, the judgment turning toward its end.
3:
"Ye have compassed this mountain long enough: turn you northward." The witnesses note the command to turn north — they had circled Seir long enough. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was time to be gone from thence, as from Horeb. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the turn northward, the people to pass the coast of their brethren the children of Esau. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the compassed long enough — the appointed measure fulfilled; the turn you northward — the advance commanded; the the discipline timed — neither endless nor purposeless; the sent forward — when its purpose was fulfilled; the God's bounds — a measure set to the trial.

The witnesses agree the word "long enough" marks God's appointed bounds to the wilderness discipline: the years of compassing Seir had served their purpose, and now the people were to turn and advance; the phrase teaches that God sets a measure even to the trials and delays of his people, and when the time is fulfilled bids them move on — the same God who held them in the desert now sending them forward, his discipline neither endless nor purposeless but timed to his wise ends.

So the witnesses make the verse long enough: The witnesses note the compassed long enough: the appointed measure fulfilled. The witnesses note the turn you northward: the advance commanded. The witnesses note the the discipline timed: neither endless nor purposeless. The witnesses note the sent forward: when its purpose was fulfilled. The witnesses note the God's bounds: a measure set to the trial. The expositors observe the compassed long enough: the appointed measure fulfilled. The expositors observe the turn you northward: the advance commanded. The expositors observe the the discipline timed: neither endless nor purposeless. The expositors observe the sent forward: when its purpose was fulfilled. The expositors observe the God's bounds: a measure set to the trial. The believer is taught to mark the compassed long enough, the turn you northward, the the discipline timed, the sent forward, the God's bounds, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that God said they had compassed the mountain long enough and bade them turn north — his discipline timed and bounded, sending his people forward when its purpose was fulfilled.
4:
"And command thou the people, saying, Ye are to pass through the coast of your brethren the children of Esau, which dwell in Seir; and they shall be afraid of you: take ye good heed unto yourselves therefore:" The witnesses note Israel charged to pass peaceably by Edom — their brethren, the children of Esau — who would fear them. The witnesses note that Gill notes a strict charge given to the people. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes this was said in view of the request to pass through (Num. 20:17), Edom being their brethren, the children of Esau, who would be afraid of them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the coast of Esau — their brethren in Seir; the they shall be afraid — edom fearing Israel; the take good heed — not to provoke them; the power restrained — strength governed by duty and the bond of kin.

The witnesses agree the charge bound Israel to a careful self-restraint toward their kinsmen: passing by Edom, the children of Esau and so their brethren, they were to "take good heed" not to provoke them, though Edom feared them and they had the power; the command to watch themselves shows that strength is to be governed by duty, and that even where they might have prevailed, the bond of brotherhood and the word of God restrained them — power held in check by reverence and right.

So the witnesses make the verse pass by Edom: The witnesses note the the coast of Esau: their brethren in Seir. The witnesses note the they shall be afraid: Edom fearing Israel. The witnesses note the take good heed: not to provoke them. The witnesses note the power restrained: strength governed by duty and the bond of kin. The expositors observe the the coast of Esau: their brethren in Seir. The expositors observe the they shall be afraid: Edom fearing Israel. The expositors observe the take good heed: not to provoke them. The expositors observe the power restrained: strength governed by duty and the bond of kin. The believer is taught to mark the the coast of Esau, the they shall be afraid, the take good heed, the power restrained, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel was charged to pass carefully by Edom, their brethren, who feared them — strength governed by duty, power held in check by reverence and the bond of brotherhood.
5:
"Meddle not with them; for I will not give you of their land, no, not so much as a foot breadth; because I have given mount Seir unto Esau for a possession." The witnesses note God forbidding Israel to take any of Edom's land, which he had given to Esau. The witnesses note that Clarke notes "meddle not with them," that is, the Edomites. The witnesses note that Ellicott marks it worthy of notice that Esau's inheritance was won earlier than Jacob's, as Ishmael's development preceded Isaac's (Gen. 25:16). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the meddle not — take none of Edom's land; the not a foot breadth — none of it for Israel; the given mount Seir to Esau — a possession granted by God; the God apportions the earth — dividing to the nations their inheritance (Deut. 32:8).

The witnesses agree the prohibition reveals God's sovereign and faithful disposal of the nations' lands: not a foot of Seir was Israel to take, for God himself had given it to Esau — a possession granted to the kin of the covenant outside the covenant line; the lesson is that the LORD apportions the earth to all peoples as he wills, "the Most High... divided to the nations their inheritance" (Deut. 32:8), and his own people must respect the bounds he has set for others, not coveting what he has given away.

So the witnesses make the verse Seir given to Esau: The witnesses note the meddle not: take none of Edom's land. The witnesses note the not a foot breadth: none of it for Israel. The witnesses note the given mount Seir to Esau: a possession granted by God. The witnesses note the God apportions the earth: dividing to the nations their inheritance (Deut. 32:8). The expositors observe the meddle not: take none of Edom's land. The expositors observe the not a foot breadth: none of it for Israel. The expositors observe the given mount Seir to Esau: a possession granted by God. The expositors observe the God apportions the earth: dividing to the nations their inheritance (Deut. 32:8). The believer is taught to mark the meddle not, the not a foot breadth, the given mount Seir to Esau, the God apportions the earth, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel was to take no foot of Seir, given by God to Esau — the LORD's sovereign disposal of the nations' lands, his people respecting the bounds he set for others (Deut. 32:8).
6:
"Ye shall buy meat of them for money, that ye may eat; and ye shall also buy water of them for money, that ye may drink." The witnesses note Israel to pay for any food or water they took of Edom, not to seize it. The witnesses note that Gill notes that though they had manna daily, they might buy other food if they would, when they had opportunity, but were not to take it by force or stealth — they were able to pay. The witnesses note that they were to buy water also for money. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the buy meat for money — paying for their food; the buy water for money — taking nothing by force; the though they had manna — provided already from heaven; the honest dealing — power no licence for plunder (Rom. 12:17).

The witnesses agree the command to buy, not seize, taught Israel honesty and restraint even toward those they could have overpowered: though they had manna from heaven, what they took of Edom they were to pay for, dealing justly with their neighbours and not abusing their strength; the rule shows that the people of God are to be upright in their dealings with all men, taking nothing by force or fraud but rendering what is due, their power no licence for plunder (cf. Rom. 12:17, provide things honest).

So the witnesses make the verse buy, not seize: The witnesses note the buy meat for money: paying for their food. The witnesses note the buy water for money: taking nothing by force. The witnesses note the though they had manna: provided already from heaven. The witnesses note the honest dealing: power no licence for plunder (Rom. 12:17). The expositors observe the buy meat for money: paying for their food. The expositors observe the buy water for money: taking nothing by force. The expositors observe the though they had manna: provided already from heaven. The expositors observe the honest dealing: power no licence for plunder (Rom. 12:17). The believer is taught to mark the buy meat for money, the buy water for money, the though they had manna, the honest dealing, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel was to buy food and water of Edom, not seize it — honest dealing toward their neighbours, the people of God taking nothing by force, their power no licence for plunder (Rom. 12:17).
7:
"For the LORD thy God hath blessed thee in all the works of thy hand: he knoweth thy walking through this great wilderness: these forty years the LORD thy God hath been with thee; thou hast lacked nothing." The witnesses note the ground of the command — God had so blessed them that they lacked nothing in forty years, and needed not to plunder. The witnesses note that Gill notes God had increased their cattle and substance, even in a wilderness. The witnesses note that Clarke notes God had given them much property, gold and silver to buy what they needed, so he would not permit them to plunder. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the blessed in all — the works of their hand; the he knoweth thy walking — god watching their every step; the forty years with thee — his presence through the wilderness; the lacked nothing — his faithful provision (Matt. 6:33).

The witnesses agree this verse is one of Scripture's great testimonies to God's faithful provision: through forty years in a great wilderness the LORD had been with his people and they had "lacked nothing," blessed in all the works of their hands; the abundance was both the reason they need not plunder and a witness to God's tender care, who knew their every step and supplied their every want — a comfort that the God who kept Israel in the desert will provide for his own, that seeking first his kingdom, they shall not lack (cf. Matt. 6:33).

So the witnesses make the verse lacked nothing: The witnesses note the blessed in all: the works of their hand. The witnesses note the he knoweth thy walking: God watching their every step. The witnesses note the forty years with thee: his presence through the wilderness. The witnesses note the lacked nothing: his faithful provision (Matt. 6:33). The expositors observe the blessed in all: the works of their hand. The expositors observe the he knoweth thy walking: God watching their every step. The expositors observe the forty years with thee: his presence through the wilderness. The expositors observe the lacked nothing: his faithful provision (Matt. 6:33). The believer is taught to mark the blessed in all, the he knoweth thy walking, the forty years with thee, the lacked nothing, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that God had been with Israel forty years so they lacked nothing — his faithful provision, the God who kept them in the wilderness providing for his own, that they need not plunder (Matt. 6:33).
8:
"And when we passed by from our brethren the children of Esau, which dwelt in Seir, through the way of the plain from Elath, and from Eziongaber, we turned and passed by the way of the wilderness of Moab." The witnesses note Israel passing peaceably by Edom and turning toward the wilderness of Moab. The witnesses note that Gill notes they passed along the coasts and borders of Esau's country. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them passing by Edom through the way of the plain from Elath and Eziongaber, then turning to the wilderness of Moab. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the passed by Esau — peaceably by their brethren; the through the plain — from Elath and Eziongaber; the turned toward Moab — as God directed; the the charge kept — the people walking in the appointed way.

The witnesses agree the verse records the obedient outworking of the command: Israel did indeed pass peaceably by their brethren of Edom, taking nothing and provoking nothing, and turned away toward Moab as God directed; the quiet keeping of the charge — passing by without conflict — shows the people walking in the way appointed, their movements ordered by God's word, the long march advancing step by step under his guidance toward the land of promise.

So the witnesses make the verse passing by Edom: The witnesses note the passed by Esau: peaceably by their brethren. The witnesses note the through the plain: from Elath and Eziongaber. The witnesses note the turned toward Moab: as God directed. The witnesses note the the charge kept: the people walking in the appointed way. The expositors observe the passed by Esau: peaceably by their brethren. The expositors observe the through the plain: from Elath and Eziongaber. The expositors observe the turned toward Moab: as God directed. The expositors observe the the charge kept: the people walking in the appointed way. The believer is taught to mark the passed by Esau, the through the plain, the turned toward Moab, the the charge kept, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel passed peaceably by Edom and turned toward Moab — the command obediently kept, their march ordered by God's word toward the land of promise.
9:
"And the LORD said unto me, Distress not the Moabites, neither contend with them in battle: for I will not give thee of their land for a possession; because I have given Ar unto the children of Lot for a possession." The witnesses note God forbidding Israel to war with Moab, whose land he had given to the children of Lot. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was spoken when Israel was upon the borders of Moab. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the command — distress not Moab, for Ar was given to the children of Lot for a possession. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the distress not Moab — no war against them; the not give thee their land — none of it for Israel; the Ar to the children of Lot — given by God to Lot's line; the faithful to old ties — honouring the kin of the covenant.

The witnesses agree the same principle that spared Edom now spared Moab: Israel was to take none of Moab's land, for God had given Ar to the children of Lot — Abraham's nephew, kin to the covenant though outside it; the LORD's faithfulness reached even to Lot's descendants, honouring the old tie, and his sovereign disposal of lands again restrained his people from coveting what he had granted to another — a fresh proof that God governs the inheritances of all peoples, not Israel only.

So the witnesses make the verse Moab spared: The witnesses note the distress not Moab: no war against them. The witnesses note the not give thee their land: none of it for Israel. The witnesses note the Ar to the children of Lot: given by God to Lot's line. The witnesses note the faithful to old ties: honouring the kin of the covenant. The expositors observe the distress not Moab: no war against them. The expositors observe the not give thee their land: none of it for Israel. The expositors observe the Ar to the children of Lot: given by God to Lot's line. The expositors observe the faithful to old ties: honouring the kin of the covenant. The believer is taught to mark the distress not Moab, the not give thee their land, the Ar to the children of Lot, the faithful to old ties, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel was to take none of Moab's land, given to the children of Lot — God's faithfulness reaching to Lot's line, his sovereign disposal restraining his people from what he granted another.
10:
"The Emims dwelt therein in times past, a people great, and many, and tall, as the Anakims;" The witnesses note that giants — the Emims — had once dwelt in Moab's land before the children of Lot possessed it. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Emims are read of as early as the times of Chedorlaomer, dwelling in Kirjathaim, a city later given to Reuben. The witnesses note that they were a people great, many, and tall as the Anakims. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Emims dwelt there — giants in times past; the great, many, and tall — as the dread Anakims; the dispossessed — driven out to make room for Moab; the God removes the mighty — clearing giants at his pleasure.

The witnesses agree the note of the Emims serves to magnify God's power in dispossessing nations: where now the children of Lot dwelt, a race of giants had once been, great and tall as the dread Anakim, yet driven out to make room for Moab; the history teaches that God removes even the mightiest peoples at his pleasure and plants others in their stead — an encouragement to Israel, that he who cleared the giants before Lot's sons could surely clear the Anakim of Canaan before them.

So the witnesses make the verse the Emims of old: The witnesses note the the Emims dwelt there: giants in times past. The witnesses note the great, many, and tall: as the dread Anakims. The witnesses note the dispossessed: driven out to make room for Moab. The witnesses note the God removes the mighty: clearing giants at his pleasure. The expositors observe the the Emims dwelt there: giants in times past. The expositors observe the great, many, and tall: as the dread Anakims. The expositors observe the dispossessed: driven out to make room for Moab. The expositors observe the God removes the mighty: clearing giants at his pleasure. The believer is taught to mark the the Emims dwelt there, the great, many, and tall, the dispossessed, the God removes the mighty, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that giants, the Emims, had dwelt in Moab's land before the children of Lot — God dispossessing even the mightiest at his pleasure, an encouragement that he could clear the Anakim before Israel.
11:
"Which also were accounted giants, as the Anakims; but the Moabites call them Emims." The witnesses note the Emims reckoned among the Rephaim, the giants, as the Anakims were. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were reckoned Rephaim, a name for giants in early times, even as the Anakims were. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the word is not "giants" but Rephaim, a particular people. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the accounted giants — among the Rephaim; the as the Anakims — peoples of great stature; the called Emims by Moab — the same race, diverse names; the swept away — no might standing when God removes a people.

The witnesses agree the naming of the Emims among the Rephaim underlines that the land had been held by a formidable race: whatever the various names — Emims, Anakims, Rephaim — these were peoples of great stature and might, and yet had been swept away before Lot's children; the recurring mention of giants dispossessed runs as a quiet theme through the chapter, that no strength of man can stand when God determines to remove a people, the mightiest as grass before him.

So the witnesses make the verse reckoned giants: The witnesses note the accounted giants: among the Rephaim. The witnesses note the as the Anakims: peoples of great stature. The witnesses note the called Emims by Moab: the same race, diverse names. The witnesses note the swept away: no might standing when God removes a people. The expositors observe the accounted giants: among the Rephaim. The expositors observe the as the Anakims: peoples of great stature. The expositors observe the called Emims by Moab: the same race, diverse names. The expositors observe the swept away: no might standing when God removes a people. The believer is taught to mark the accounted giants, the as the Anakims, the called Emims by Moab, the swept away, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that the Emims were reckoned giants, as the Anakims — peoples of great might swept away before Lot's children, no strength of man standing when God removes a nation.
12:
"The Horims also dwelt in Seir beforetime; but the children of Esau succeeded them... as Israel did unto the land of his possession, which the LORD gave unto them." The witnesses note the Horims who once dwelt in Seir, dispossessed by Esau — as Israel would dispossess Canaan. The witnesses note that Gill notes Seir was named from Seir the Horite who dwelt there before Esau; the Horim seem so called from dwelling in holes and caves. The witnesses note that the children of Esau succeeded them, as Israel did the land the LORD gave them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Horims in Seir — dwellers in caves of old; the the children of Esau succeeded — dispossessing them; the as Israel would do — to the land God gave; the the precedent a pledge — their conquest on the same sure ground.

The witnesses agree the comparison drawn in the verse is full of encouragement: as Esau's children had driven out the Horims and taken Seir, so Israel would dispossess the nations of Canaan and possess the land God gave them; the parallel set plainly before them — the same God who gave Seir to Esau giving Canaan to Israel — assured the people that their conquest stood on the same sure ground of divine gift, the precedent of nations dispossessed a pledge of their own inheritance.

So the witnesses make the verse the Horims dispossessed: The witnesses note the the Horims in Seir: dwellers in caves of old. The witnesses note the the children of Esau succeeded: dispossessing them. The witnesses note the as Israel would do: to the land God gave. The witnesses note the the precedent a pledge: their conquest on the same sure ground. The expositors observe the the Horims in Seir: dwellers in caves of old. The expositors observe the the children of Esau succeeded: dispossessing them. The expositors observe the as Israel would do: to the land God gave. The expositors observe the the precedent a pledge: their conquest on the same sure ground. The believer is taught to mark the the Horims in Seir, the the children of Esau succeeded, the as Israel would do, the the precedent a pledge, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Esau's children dispossessed the Horims of Seir, as Israel would Canaan — the precedent of nations dispossessed a pledge of Israel's inheritance, given on the same sure ground of divine gift.
13:
"Now rise up, said I, and get you over the brook Zered. And we went over the brook Zered." The witnesses note the command to cross the brook Zered, and Israel's crossing — a marked stage of the journey. The witnesses note that Gill notes it is called the valley of Zered, the word meaning both valley and brook, near Dibongad. The witnesses note that they rose and went over as commanded. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rise up — the command to move; the over the brook Zered — a marked boundary crossed; the we went over — obeying at God's word; the a fixed stage — the journey moving by his appointment.

The witnesses agree the crossing of Zered marked a fixed boundary in the long march, the point at which one era of the journey closed and another opened: at God's word they rose and passed over, a definite step forward after the years of wandering; the crossing, soon to be named as the term of the thirty-eight years, shows the journey moving by God's appointed stages, each advance taken at his command, the people pressing on toward the land as he led them.

So the witnesses make the verse crossing Zered: The witnesses note the rise up: the command to move. The witnesses note the over the brook Zered: a marked boundary crossed. The witnesses note the we went over: obeying at God's word. The witnesses note the a fixed stage: the journey moving by his appointment. The expositors observe the rise up: the command to move. The expositors observe the over the brook Zered: a marked boundary crossed. The expositors observe the we went over: obeying at God's word. The expositors observe the a fixed stage: the journey moving by his appointment. The believer is taught to mark the rise up, the over the brook Zered, the we went over, the a fixed stage, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel crossed the brook Zered at God's command — a marked stage of the journey, the people pressing on by his appointed stages toward the land.
14:
"And the space in which we came from Kadesh-barnea, until we were come over the brook Zered, was thirty and eight years; until all the generation of the men of war were wasted out from among the host, as the LORD sware unto them." The witnesses note the thirty-eight years between Kadesh and Zered, in which the whole generation of war-men was wasted out, as God had sworn. The witnesses note that Gill notes the space — the number of the days, or years. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the thirty-eight years until all the generation of the men of war were wasted out, as the LORD sware. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the thirty-eight years — from Kadesh to Zered; the the men of war wasted — the unbelieving generation dying off; the as the LORD sware — his oath against unbelief; the fulfilled to the letter — the carcasses fallen in the desert (Heb. 3:17).

The witnesses agree the verse fixes the fulfilment of God's oath: the thirty-eight years from Kadesh to Zered were exactly the time in which the unbelieving generation died off, the whole army of those who had refused the land wasting away in the wilderness as God had sworn; the precise correspondence — the years measured by the wasting of that generation — is a solemn seal upon the truth of God's threatenings, his oath against unbelief fulfilled to the letter, the carcasses fallen in the desert (cf. Heb. 3:17).

So the witnesses make the verse the wasting generation: The witnesses note the thirty-eight years: from Kadesh to Zered. The witnesses note the the men of war wasted: the unbelieving generation dying off. The witnesses note the as the LORD sware: his oath against unbelief. The witnesses note the fulfilled to the letter: the carcasses fallen in the desert (Heb. 3:17). The expositors observe the thirty-eight years: from Kadesh to Zered. The expositors observe the the men of war wasted: the unbelieving generation dying off. The expositors observe the as the LORD sware: his oath against unbelief. The expositors observe the fulfilled to the letter: the carcasses fallen in the desert (Heb. 3:17). The believer is taught to mark the thirty-eight years, the the men of war wasted, the as the LORD sware, the fulfilled to the letter, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that the thirty-eight years saw the whole generation of war-men wasted out, as God had sworn — his oath against unbelief fulfilled to the letter, the carcasses fallen in the wilderness (Heb. 3:17).
15:
"For indeed the hand of the LORD was against them, to destroy them from among the host, until they were consumed." The witnesses note the hand of the LORD against that generation, consuming them for their unbelief. The witnesses note that Gill notes God's power was exerted in wrath and vengeance for their murmuring at the report of the spies; strong is his hand, and when lifted up it falls heavy, smiting them with one disease or another. The witnesses note that they were consumed from among the host. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the hand of the LORD — his active judgment; the against them — for their murmuring unbelief; the to destroy them — consuming by sickness and stroke; the a fearful warning — none standing when his hand is lifted in wrath.

The witnesses agree the wasting was no mere course of nature but the active judgment of God: "the hand of the LORD was against them," consuming the rebellious generation by sickness and stroke until none was left; the heavy hand of God upon the unbelieving is a fearful warning, that he who is gracious to the faithful is terrible against those who despise his word, and that none can stand when his hand is lifted up in wrath — a sobering witness to the wages of unbelief.

So the witnesses make the verse the hand of the LORD: The witnesses note the the hand of the LORD: his active judgment. The witnesses note the against them: for their murmuring unbelief. The witnesses note the to destroy them: consuming by sickness and stroke. The witnesses note the a fearful warning: none standing when his hand is lifted in wrath. The expositors observe the the hand of the LORD: his active judgment. The expositors observe the against them: for their murmuring unbelief. The expositors observe the to destroy them: consuming by sickness and stroke. The expositors observe the a fearful warning: none standing when his hand is lifted in wrath. The believer is taught to mark the the hand of the LORD, the against them, the to destroy them, the a fearful warning, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that the hand of the LORD was against that generation to consume them — the active judgment of God on unbelief, a fearful warning that none can stand when his hand is lifted in wrath.
16:
"So it came to pass, when all the men of war were consumed and dead from among the people," The witnesses note the point reached when the whole generation of war-men was dead and consumed. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were consumed by wasting diseases and judgments of one kind or another. The witnesses note that the old generation was now wholly gone from among the people. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the men of war — the old fighting generation; the consumed and dead — the judgment complete; the from among the people — wholly passed away; the a new chapter — opening for the people; the a turning point — the promise carried to the children.

The witnesses agree the verse marks a turning point, the old generation now wholly passed away: with the men of war dead, the judgment was complete and a new chapter could open for the people; the clearing-out of the unbelieving host before the fresh advance shows that God's purposes go forward through the passing of one generation and the rising of another, the promise carried on to the children when the fathers, who forfeited it, were gone.

So the witnesses make the verse the generation gone: The witnesses note the the men of war: the old fighting generation. The witnesses note the consumed and dead: the judgment complete. The witnesses note the from among the people: wholly passed away. The witnesses note the a new chapter: opening for the people. The witnesses note the a turning point: the promise carried to the children. The expositors observe the the men of war: the old fighting generation. The expositors observe the consumed and dead: the judgment complete. The expositors observe the from among the people: wholly passed away. The expositors observe the a new chapter: opening for the people. The expositors observe the a turning point: the promise carried to the children. The believer is taught to mark the the men of war, the consumed and dead, the from among the people, the a new chapter, the a turning point, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that the whole generation of war-men was now dead and consumed — the judgment complete, the promise carried on to the children as the fathers who forfeited it passed away.
17:
"That the LORD spake unto me, saying," The witnesses note God speaking again to Moses, now to direct the passage by Ammon. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was at the brook Zered, having passed it, or at Dibongad, their next station. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the word — to pass through Ar, the coast of Moab, and to distress not Ammon. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD spake — to Moses once more; the after the judgment — the old generation gone; the to guide past Ammon — the new stage of the march; the his word leading on — the new generation directed.

The witnesses agree the renewed word of the LORD, now the old generation was gone, opened the new stage of the march: God spoke again to guide the people past Ammon as he had past Edom and Moab; the resuming of the divine direction marks the fresh beginning after the judgment, the LORD taking up his guidance of the new generation, leading them on by his word toward the lands they should and should not possess.

So the witnesses make the verse God speaks again: The witnesses note the the LORD spake: to Moses once more. The witnesses note the after the judgment: the old generation gone. The witnesses note the to guide past Ammon: the new stage of the march. The witnesses note the his word leading on: the new generation directed. The expositors observe the the LORD spake: to Moses once more. The expositors observe the after the judgment: the old generation gone. The expositors observe the to guide past Ammon: the new stage of the march. The expositors observe the his word leading on: the new generation directed. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD spake, the after the judgment, the to guide past Ammon, the his word leading on, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that the LORD spake again to Moses, the old generation now gone — taking up his guidance of the new, leading them on by his word past Ammon.
18:
"Thou art to pass over through Ar, the coast of Moab, this day:" The witnesses note Israel to pass that day by Ar, the border of Moab. The witnesses note that Gill notes this means passing over the river Arnon, by the city Ar of Moab situated by it. The witnesses note that Ellicott identifies Ar with Rabbath-Moab, the present ruin Rabba. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the pass over through Ar — by the border of Moab; the this day — the march now quickened; the toward the Amorite — the land to be possessed; the the wandering over — the long detour behind them; the step by step — each ordered by the LORD who led them.

The witnesses agree the brief direction marks the steady advance of the people along the appointed way: passing by Ar on Moab's border, they moved forward at God's word toward the territory of the Amorite they were to possess; the naming of the day — "this day" — shows the march now quickened, the long wandering over and the people pressing on stage by stage, each step ordered by the LORD who led them toward the land.

So the witnesses make the verse passing Ar: The witnesses note the pass over through Ar: by the border of Moab. The witnesses note the this day: the march now quickened. The witnesses note the toward the Amorite: the land to be possessed. The witnesses note the the wandering over: the long detour behind them. The witnesses note the step by step: each ordered by the LORD who led them. The expositors observe the pass over through Ar: by the border of Moab. The expositors observe the this day: the march now quickened. The expositors observe the toward the Amorite: the land to be possessed. The expositors observe the the wandering over: the long detour behind them. The expositors observe the step by step: each ordered by the LORD who led them. The believer is taught to mark the pass over through Ar, the this day, the toward the Amorite, the the wandering over, the step by step, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel was to pass by Ar of Moab that day — the steady advance ordered by God, the long wandering over, the people pressing on toward the land.
19:
"And when thou comest nigh over against the children of Ammon, distress them not, nor meddle with them: for I will not give thee of the land of the children of Ammon any possession; because I have given it unto the children of Lot for a possession." The witnesses note God forbidding Israel to war with Ammon, whose land he had given to the children of Lot. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Ammonites dwelt near the Moabites and were brethren, both descending from Lot. The witnesses note that Calvin notes God now makes provision for the Ammonites, their condition the same as the Moabites', being descended from Lot. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the distress them not — no war against Ammon; the not give thee their land — none of it for Israel; the given to the children of Lot — for the sake of that kinship; the God's sovereign disposal — apportioning each people its bounds (Acts 17:26).

The reformed reader (Calvin) marks that the same grace covered Ammon as Moab: both descended from Lot, and so for the sake of that kinship God had given them their land and forbade Israel to touch it; the LORD's faithfulness to the seed of Lot, honouring the tie to Abraham's nephew, shows the breadth of his kindness and the constancy of his sovereign disposal — apportioning to each people its inheritance and binding his own to respect the bounds he set, that they covet not what he has given to another (cf. Acts 17:26).

So the witnesses make the verse Ammon spared: The witnesses note the distress them not: no war against Ammon. The witnesses note the not give thee their land: none of it for Israel. The witnesses note the given to the children of Lot: for the sake of that kinship. The witnesses note the God's sovereign disposal: apportioning each people its bounds (Acts 17:26). The expositors observe the distress them not: no war against Ammon. The expositors observe the not give thee their land: none of it for Israel. The expositors observe the given to the children of Lot: for the sake of that kinship. The expositors observe the God's sovereign disposal: apportioning each people its bounds (Acts 17:26). The believer is taught to mark the distress them not, the not give thee their land, the given to the children of Lot, the God's sovereign disposal, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel was to take none of Ammon's land, given to the children of Lot — God's faithfulness to Lot's seed, his sovereign disposal apportioning each people its inheritance (Acts 17:26).
20:
"(That also was accounted a land of giants: giants dwelt therein in old time; and the Ammonites call them Zamzummims;" The witnesses note Ammon's land also once held by giants — the Zamzummims. The witnesses note that Gill notes Ammon was reckoned a land of giants, as Moab was. The witnesses note that Clarke takes the Zamzummims to be the same as the Zuzim (Gen. 14:5), of whom little is known. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a land of giants — as Moab was reckoned; the the Zamzummims — the giants of Ammon of old; the the same as the Zuzim — of whom little is known (Gen. 14:5); the giants dispossessed — god settling the nations, not man's strength.

The witnesses agree the note of the Zamzummims continues the chapter's steady theme of giants dispossessed: Ammon too had once been a land of mighty men, yet they had been removed to make room for Lot's children; the repeated record of formidable races driven out before weaker peoples teaches that God, not human strength, settles the nations, and prepares Israel to trust that the same hand which cleared giants for Ammon would clear the Anakim for them.

So the witnesses make the verse the Zamzummims: The witnesses note the a land of giants: as Moab was reckoned. The witnesses note the the Zamzummims: the giants of Ammon of old. The witnesses note the the same as the Zuzim: of whom little is known (Gen. 14:5). The witnesses note the giants dispossessed: God settling the nations, not man's strength. The expositors observe the a land of giants: as Moab was reckoned. The expositors observe the the Zamzummims: the giants of Ammon of old. The expositors observe the the same as the Zuzim: of whom little is known (Gen. 14:5). The expositors observe the giants dispossessed: God settling the nations, not man's strength. The believer is taught to mark the a land of giants, the the Zamzummims, the the same as the Zuzim, the giants dispossessed, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Ammon's land too had held giants, the Zamzummims — the steady theme of mighty races dispossessed, God and not human strength settling the nations.
21:
"A people great, and many, and tall, as the Anakims; but the LORD destroyed them before them; and they succeeded them, and dwelt in their stead:" The witnesses note the LORD destroying the Zamzummim giants before the Ammonites, who took their place. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Lord destroyed the Zamzummims before the children of Ammon, who would otherwise have been too much for them, being so numerous and of gigantic stature. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the conquest of Canaan is here, as it were, begun, God destroying nations before others. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the great and tall — as the Anakims; the the LORD destroyed them — before the Ammonites; the they succeeded them — dwelling in their stead; the faith's ground — no greatness standing when God fights.

The witnesses agree the verse states plainly that it was the LORD who dispossessed the giants for Ammon: a people great and tall as the Anakim were destroyed before the children of Lot, not by their own might but by God's; the lesson, pressed home for Israel, is that no greatness of the enemy can stand when God fights against them, and that the conquest of Canaan, where Anakim also dwelt, was already foreshadowed in these victories God wrought for others — a sure ground for faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD destroyed them: The witnesses note the great and tall: as the Anakims. The witnesses note the the LORD destroyed them: before the Ammonites. The witnesses note the they succeeded them: dwelling in their stead. The witnesses note the faith's ground: no greatness standing when God fights. The expositors observe the great and tall: as the Anakims. The expositors observe the the LORD destroyed them: before the Ammonites. The expositors observe the they succeeded them: dwelling in their stead. The expositors observe the faith's ground: no greatness standing when God fights. The believer is taught to mark the great and tall, the the LORD destroyed them, the they succeeded them, the faith's ground, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that the LORD destroyed the giants before Ammon, who took their place — no greatness of the enemy standing when God fights, the conquest of Canaan already foreshadowed for Israel's faith.
22:
"As he did to the children of Esau, which dwelt in Seir, when he destroyed the Horims from before them; and they succeeded them, and dwelt in their stead even unto this day:" The witnesses note the same done for Esau — God destroying the Horims before them, that they might dwell in their stead. The witnesses note that Gill notes God did the like for the children of Esau as for the Ammonites. The witnesses note that they succeeded the Horims and dwelt there to this day. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the as for the children of Esau — the like done for them; the destroyed the Horims — before Esau's children; the they succeeded them — dwelling in their stead; the God's providence — the rise and fall of peoples his work.

The witnesses agree the parallel cases are heaped up to one end — to show God's hand in every settlement of the nations: as he cleared the Horims for Esau and the giants for Ammon, so he ordered the dwelling-places of all; the repeated pattern, "he destroyed... they succeeded," teaches that the rise and fall of peoples is the work of God's providence, and assures Israel that the God who had so often dispossessed the strong for others would do the same for them in Canaan.

So the witnesses make the verse as for Esau: The witnesses note the as for the children of Esau: the like done for them. The witnesses note the destroyed the Horims: before Esau's children. The witnesses note the they succeeded them: dwelling in their stead. The witnesses note the God's providence: the rise and fall of peoples his work. The expositors observe the as for the children of Esau: the like done for them. The expositors observe the destroyed the Horims: before Esau's children. The expositors observe the they succeeded them: dwelling in their stead. The expositors observe the God's providence: the rise and fall of peoples his work. The believer is taught to mark the as for the children of Esau, the destroyed the Horims, the they succeeded them, the God's providence, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that God destroyed the Horims before Esau, as the giants before Ammon — the rise and fall of peoples the work of his providence, a pledge of the like for Israel in Canaan.
23:
"And the Avims which dwelt in Hazerim, even unto Azzah, the Caphtorims, which came forth out of Caphtor, destroyed them, and dwelt in their stead.)" The witnesses note the Avims, near Gaza, destroyed by the Caphtorims, who dwelt in their place. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Avims are the same as the Avites, who dwelt in part of the land of the Philistines, where Azzah (Gaza) was; Hazerim signifies courts, tents or huts set in a square. The witnesses note that the Caphtorims came forth out of Caphtor and destroyed them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Avims near Gaza — dwelling in Hazerim unto Azzah; the the Caphtorims — out of Caphtor; the destroyed them — dwelling in their stead; the God's ordering — the bounds of all habitation his (Acts 17:26).

The witnesses agree this further instance rounds out the theme: even among the heathen peoples, one nation displaced another — the Caphtorims driving out the Avims near Gaza — all under the ordering of God who governs the migrations and dwellings of all men; the example shows that the whole earth's peopling is no chance but God's disposing, "who hath determined the times before appointed, and the bounds of their habitation" (Acts 17:26), the same providence that ruled the heathen ruling for Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse the Avims displaced: The witnesses note the the Avims near Gaza: dwelling in Hazerim unto Azzah. The witnesses note the the Caphtorims: out of Caphtor. The witnesses note the destroyed them: dwelling in their stead. The witnesses note the God's ordering: the bounds of all habitation his (Acts 17:26). The expositors observe the the Avims near Gaza: dwelling in Hazerim unto Azzah. The expositors observe the the Caphtorims: out of Caphtor. The expositors observe the destroyed them: dwelling in their stead. The expositors observe the God's ordering: the bounds of all habitation his (Acts 17:26). The believer is taught to mark the the Avims near Gaza, the the Caphtorims, the destroyed them, the God's ordering, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that the Caphtorims destroyed the Avims and took their place — the migrations of all peoples under God's ordering, who sets the bounds of every habitation (Acts 17:26).
24:
"Rise ye up, take your journey, and pass over the river Arnon: behold, I have given into thine hand Sihon the Amorite, king of Heshbon, and his land: begin to possess it, and contend with him in battle." The witnesses note the command to cross Arnon and possess Sihon's land, which God had given into their hand. The witnesses note that Gill notes Arnon was on the border of Moab, dividing between Moab and the Amorites. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the command — I have given Sihon and his land into thine hand; begin to possess it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the pass over Arnon — the border crossed at last; the given into thine hand — sihon and his land; the begin to possess — the conquest now begun; the faith on God's promise — the victory pledged before the battle.

The witnesses agree the command marks the true beginning of the conquest: at Arnon, Israel was at last bidden not to pass by but to possess, God declaring Sihon already "given into thine hand"; the victory is spoken of as won before the battle, the gift sure though the contending lay ahead — teaching that God's people go to their warfare on the ground of his promise, the inheritance assured by his word and to be taken in the strength of his giving, faith laying hold of the victory God has pledged.

So the witnesses make the verse Sihon given: The witnesses note the pass over Arnon: the border crossed at last. The witnesses note the given into thine hand: Sihon and his land. The witnesses note the begin to possess: the conquest now begun. The witnesses note the faith on God's promise: the victory pledged before the battle. The expositors observe the pass over Arnon: the border crossed at last. The expositors observe the given into thine hand: Sihon and his land. The expositors observe the begin to possess: the conquest now begun. The expositors observe the faith on God's promise: the victory pledged before the battle. The believer is taught to mark the pass over Arnon, the given into thine hand, the begin to possess, the faith on God's promise, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that God bade Israel cross Arnon and possess Sihon's land, given into their hand — the conquest begun on the ground of God's promise, the victory assured by his word before the battle.
25:
"This day will I begin to put the dread of thee and the fear of thee upon the nations that are under the whole heaven, who shall hear report of thee, and shall tremble, and be in anguish because of thee." The witnesses note God beginning to put the dread of Israel upon the nations, that they should tremble at the report of them. The witnesses note that Gill notes this fulfilled the prophecies Moses had delivered (Exod. 15:14-16). The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the dread and fear put upon the nations under the whole heaven. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the this day will I begin — god going before his people; the the dread of thee — fear upon the nations; the hear and tremble — the peoples in anguish at the report; the the battle the LORD's — enemies' hearts melting before them (Josh. 2:9).

The witnesses agree God himself went before his people, striking terror into their enemies: the dread of Israel was to fall on the nations at the mere report of them, fulfilling the song at the sea that the peoples should tremble; the LORD's sending of fear before his host shows that the battle was his, the victory wrought as much by the terror he cast on the foe as by the sword of Israel — a token that when God fights for his people, the hearts of their enemies melt before them (cf. Josh. 2:9).

So the witnesses make the verse the dread of Israel: The witnesses note the this day will I begin: God going before his people. The witnesses note the the dread of thee: fear upon the nations. The witnesses note the hear and tremble: the peoples in anguish at the report. The witnesses note the the battle the LORD's: enemies' hearts melting before them (Josh. 2:9). The expositors observe the this day will I begin: God going before his people. The expositors observe the the dread of thee: fear upon the nations. The expositors observe the hear and tremble: the peoples in anguish at the report. The expositors observe the the battle the LORD's: enemies' hearts melting before them (Josh. 2:9). The believer is taught to mark the this day will I begin, the the dread of thee, the hear and tremble, the the battle the LORD's, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that God began to put the dread of Israel on the nations, that they should tremble at the report — the LORD going before his host, the enemies' hearts melting, the battle his (Josh. 2:9).
26:
"And I sent messengers out of the wilderness of Kedemoth unto Sihon king of Heshbon with words of peace, saying," The witnesses note Israel first sending messengers of peace to Sihon before any battle. The witnesses note that Gill notes Kedemoth was a city later given to the Levites, near a wilderness named from it. The witnesses note that the messengers came with words of peace. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sent messengers — from the wilderness of Kedemoth; the to Sihon — the Amorite king; the words of peace — offering terms before war; the the righteous order — peace honestly offered first (Deut. 20:10).

The witnesses agree the sending of peaceable words before war shows the justice and moderation of Israel's cause: though God had given Sihon into their hand, they first sought a peaceful passage, offering terms before resorting to the sword; the seeking of peace first — even where victory was promised — teaches the righteous order of God's people, that war is not to be rushed into but peace honestly offered, the guilt of the conflict laid at the door of the one who refused it (cf. Deut. 20:10).

So the witnesses make the verse words of peace: The witnesses note the sent messengers: from the wilderness of Kedemoth. The witnesses note the to Sihon: the Amorite king. The witnesses note the words of peace: offering terms before war. The witnesses note the the righteous order: peace honestly offered first (Deut. 20:10). The expositors observe the sent messengers: from the wilderness of Kedemoth. The expositors observe the to Sihon: the Amorite king. The expositors observe the words of peace: offering terms before war. The expositors observe the the righteous order: peace honestly offered first (Deut. 20:10). The believer is taught to mark the sent messengers, the to Sihon, the words of peace, the the righteous order, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel sent words of peace to Sihon before battle — peace honestly offered first, the justice of their cause, the guilt of war laid at the door of the one who refused it (Deut. 20:10).
27:
"Let me pass through thy land: I will go along by the high way, I will neither turn unto the right hand nor to the left." The witnesses note the modest request — leave to pass straight through, turning neither way. The witnesses note that Gill refers to the like request made before (Num. 21:22). The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the terms — to keep the high way, neither turning right nor left. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the let me pass — only leave to go through; the by the high way — keeping to the road; the neither right nor left — trespassing on nothing; the no just cause for war — the refusal Sihon's own fault.

The witnesses agree the request was as fair and harmless as could be framed: Israel asked only to pass along the highway, promising to keep to the road and trespass on nothing, turning neither right nor left; the modesty of the petition left Sihon no just cause for war, and made his refusal the more plainly his own fault — the people seeking only passage, not plunder, and binding themselves to do no harm, that the blame of the coming conflict rest wholly on the Amorite.

So the witnesses make the verse the modest request: The witnesses note the let me pass: only leave to go through. The witnesses note the by the high way: keeping to the road. The witnesses note the neither right nor left: trespassing on nothing. The witnesses note the no just cause for war: the refusal Sihon's own fault. The expositors observe the let me pass: only leave to go through. The expositors observe the by the high way: keeping to the road. The expositors observe the neither right nor left: trespassing on nothing. The expositors observe the no just cause for war: the refusal Sihon's own fault. The believer is taught to mark the let me pass, the by the high way, the neither right nor left, the no just cause for war, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel asked only to pass by the highway, turning neither way — a fair and harmless request, leaving Sihon no just cause for war, the blame of the conflict his own.
28:
"Thou shalt sell me meat for money, that I may eat; and give me water for money, that I may drink: only I will pass through on my feet;" The witnesses note Israel offering to buy food and water and pass through peaceably on foot. The witnesses note that Gill notes they desired only to pay for provision, if Sihon thought fit to give it. The witnesses note that Wesley notes "on my feet," or with a company on foot, added significantly, since a host of horsemen might have done more mischief. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sell me meat — paying for their food; the water for money — taking nothing free; the on my feet — as foot-travellers, not horsemen; the every fear removed — the offer studiously inoffensive.

The witnesses agree the terms were studiously inoffensive: Israel would pay for all they took and pass through as foot-travellers, not as a threatening cavalry, doing the land no harm; the careful framing — buying their food, going on foot — was meant to remove every ground of suspicion or fear, that Sihon might see they came not to spoil but only to pass; the very reasonableness of the offer heightened the guilt of his refusal and the justice of the war that followed.

So the witnesses make the verse peaceable terms: The witnesses note the sell me meat: paying for their food. The witnesses note the water for money: taking nothing free. The witnesses note the on my feet: as foot-travellers, not horsemen. The witnesses note the every fear removed: the offer studiously inoffensive. The expositors observe the sell me meat: paying for their food. The expositors observe the water for money: taking nothing free. The expositors observe the on my feet: as foot-travellers, not horsemen. The expositors observe the every fear removed: the offer studiously inoffensive. The believer is taught to mark the sell me meat, the water for money, the on my feet, the every fear removed, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel offered to buy food and pass through on foot — terms studiously inoffensive, removing every ground of fear, heightening the guilt of Sihon's refusal.
29:
"(As the children of Esau which dwell in Seir, and the Moabites which dwell in Ar, did unto me;) until I shall pass over Jordan into the land which the LORD our God giveth us." The witnesses note Israel citing Edom and Moab, who had let them buy food, as precedent — their aim only to reach the land beyond Jordan. The witnesses note that Gill, with Jarchi, notes this respects not passing through their land (which neither granted) but the buying of food and drink. The witnesses note that their aim was only to pass over Jordan into the land God gave them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the as Esau and Moab — who sold them food; the did unto me — the precedent of provision; the until I pass over Jordan — their aim the land beyond; the no hostile design — seeking passage, not conquest.

The witnesses agree the appeal to precedent further cleared Israel of any hostile design: as Edom and Moab had at least sold them provision, so Sihon was asked to do no more, their whole aim being to reach the land God had given them beyond Jordan; the citing of the kinsmen's dealings showed Israel sought only safe passage, not conquest of Sihon's land — making his refusal the more unreasonable and the war upon him the more plainly just, the people pressing peaceably toward their promised inheritance.

So the witnesses make the verse the precedent cited: The witnesses note the as Esau and Moab: who sold them food. The witnesses note the did unto me: the precedent of provision. The witnesses note the until I pass over Jordan: their aim the land beyond. The witnesses note the no hostile design: seeking passage, not conquest. The expositors observe the as Esau and Moab: who sold them food. The expositors observe the did unto me: the precedent of provision. The expositors observe the until I pass over Jordan: their aim the land beyond. The expositors observe the no hostile design: seeking passage, not conquest. The believer is taught to mark the as Esau and Moab, the did unto me, the until I pass over Jordan, the no hostile design, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel cited Edom and Moab as precedent, seeking only to reach the land beyond Jordan — clearing themselves of hostile design, making Sihon's refusal the more unjust.
30:
"But Sihon king of Heshbon would not let us pass by him: for the LORD thy God hardened his spirit, and made his heart obstinate, that he might deliver him into thy hand, as appeareth this day." The witnesses note Sihon's refusal, the LORD having hardened his spirit, that he might be delivered into Israel's hand. The witnesses note that Clarke refers to the hardening as with Pharaoh (Exod. 4:21). The witnesses note that Ellicott notes Jehovah gave Sihon his strength, as he had done Pharaoh, the obstinacy serving God's purpose of judgment. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the would not let us pass — the Amorite's refusal; the the LORD hardened his spirit — as with Pharaoh (Exod. 4:21); the made his heart obstinate — given up to his own hardness; the God's sovereignty — hardening whom he will, for judgment (Rom. 9:18).

The witnesses agree this verse opens the deep matter of God's sovereignty over the wicked: Sihon refused passage because "the LORD hardened his spirit," that he might be given over to judgment — God justly leaving the proud Amorite to his own obstinacy and overruling it to his ruin; the case stands with Pharaoh's as a witness that God hardeneth whom he will (Rom. 9:18), not by forcing men to sin but by giving them up to the hardness they choose, and bending even their rebellion to serve his righteous ends, the Amorite's long-ripened iniquity now ripe for judgment.

So the witnesses make the verse Sihon hardened: The witnesses note the would not let us pass: the Amorite's refusal. The witnesses note the the LORD hardened his spirit: as with Pharaoh (Exod. 4:21). The witnesses note the made his heart obstinate: given up to his own hardness. The witnesses note the God's sovereignty: hardening whom he will, for judgment (Rom. 9:18). The expositors observe the would not let us pass: the Amorite's refusal. The expositors observe the the LORD hardened his spirit: as with Pharaoh (Exod. 4:21). The expositors observe the made his heart obstinate: given up to his own hardness. The expositors observe the God's sovereignty: hardening whom he will, for judgment (Rom. 9:18). The believer is taught to mark the would not let us pass, the the LORD hardened his spirit, the made his heart obstinate, the God's sovereignty, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Sihon refused, the LORD having hardened his spirit for judgment — God's sovereignty over the wicked, giving the proud Amorite up to his own obstinacy and bending it to his ruin (Rom. 9:18).
31:
"And the LORD said unto me, Behold, I have begun to give Sihon and his land before thee: begin to possess, that thou mayest inherit his land." The witnesses note God declaring he had begun to give Sihon's land, bidding Israel begin to possess it. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was spoken about the time the messengers had returned with Sihon's answer. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes in all Israel's conquests Jehovah gave the order to begin the attack (Deut. 7:2). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the I have begun to give — god's gift of the land; the Sihon and his land — before Israel; the begin to possess — at God's command; the the war his commission — every attack ordered by him (Deut. 7:2).

The witnesses agree the conquest proceeded wholly at God's word and gift: the LORD declared he had "begun to give" Sihon's land, and only then bade Israel begin to possess it; that God gave the order to begin every attack shows the wars of Israel were not their own enterprise but his commission, the land received as his gift and taken at his command — teaching that the people of God act rightly only as they move at his word, the inheritance both given and to be possessed by the same sovereign will.

So the witnesses make the verse begin to possess: The witnesses note the I have begun to give: God's gift of the land. The witnesses note the Sihon and his land: before Israel. The witnesses note the begin to possess: at God's command. The witnesses note the the war his commission: every attack ordered by him (Deut. 7:2). The expositors observe the I have begun to give: God's gift of the land. The expositors observe the Sihon and his land: before Israel. The expositors observe the begin to possess: at God's command. The expositors observe the the war his commission: every attack ordered by him (Deut. 7:2). The believer is taught to mark the I have begun to give, the Sihon and his land, the begin to possess, the the war his commission, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that God declared he had begun to give Sihon's land and bade Israel possess it — the conquest his gift and commission, the people moving and possessing at his sovereign word (Deut. 7:2).
32:
"Then Sihon came out against us, he and all his people, to fight at Jahaz." The witnesses note Sihon marching out with all his people to fight Israel at Jahaz. The witnesses note that Gill notes Sihon, perceiving Israel upon the march toward his land, gathered all his people and went out of Heshbon his capital. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the defeat of Sihon, as in Num. 21:23-26. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the came out against us — marching to battle; the all his people — his whole force gathered; the to fight at Jahaz — meeting Israel there; the to his own ruin — the aggressor walking into judgment.

The witnesses agree Sihon's coming out to battle sealed his own doom and fulfilled God's purpose: in his hardened pride he gathered his whole force and marched against Israel at Jahaz, walking into the judgment God had prepared; the aggressor became the victim, his obstinate refusal of peace bringing him to the very war that destroyed him — a solemn instance that the wicked, left to their own hearts, hasten to their ruin, and that God overrules their fury to accomplish his righteous ends.

So the witnesses make the verse Sihon comes out: The witnesses note the came out against us: marching to battle. The witnesses note the all his people: his whole force gathered. The witnesses note the to fight at Jahaz: meeting Israel there. The witnesses note the to his own ruin: the aggressor walking into judgment. The expositors observe the came out against us: marching to battle. The expositors observe the all his people: his whole force gathered. The expositors observe the to fight at Jahaz: meeting Israel there. The expositors observe the to his own ruin: the aggressor walking into judgment. The believer is taught to mark the came out against us, the all his people, the to fight at Jahaz, the to his own ruin, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Sihon came out with all his people to fight at Jahaz — the hardened aggressor walking into the judgment God had prepared, his fury overruled to his own ruin.
33:
"And the LORD our God delivered him before us; and we smote him, and his sons, and all his people." The witnesses note the LORD delivering Sihon before Israel, who smote him, his sons, and all his people. The witnesses note that Gill notes the LORD delivered him with their lands before them. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Israel smiting Sihon, his sons, and all his people. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD delivered him — the victory God's gift; the we smote him — the overthrow complete; the his sons and people — none escaping; the the promise fulfilled — what God pledged performed in fact.

The witnesses agree the victory is ascribed wholly to God: "the LORD our God delivered him before us," the triumph his gift and not Israel's strength; the complete overthrow of Sihon, his sons, and his people was the promised deliverance made good, the word "I have given him into thine hand" now fulfilled in fact — teaching that what God promises he performs, and that his people's victories are to be owned as his doing, the glory of the conquest given to the LORD who fought for them.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD delivered him: The witnesses note the the LORD delivered him: the victory God's gift. The witnesses note the we smote him: the overthrow complete. The witnesses note the his sons and people: none escaping. The witnesses note the the promise fulfilled: what God pledged performed in fact. The expositors observe the the LORD delivered him: the victory God's gift. The expositors observe the we smote him: the overthrow complete. The expositors observe the his sons and people: none escaping. The expositors observe the the promise fulfilled: what God pledged performed in fact. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD delivered him, the we smote him, the his sons and people, the the promise fulfilled, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that the LORD delivered Sihon before Israel, who smote him utterly — the victory God's gift, the promise fulfilled in fact, the glory of the conquest his.
34:
"And we took all his cities at that time, and utterly destroyed the men, and the women, and the little ones, of every city, we left none to remain:" The witnesses note Israel taking all Sihon's cities and utterly destroying their people, leaving none. The witnesses note that Gill notes they took all his cities, Heshbon and others. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes "utterly destroyed" means devoted to destruction, made cherem like the spoil of Jericho — this could only be by divine direction, the word implying nothing less. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the took all his cities — heshbon and the rest; the utterly destroyed — devoted as cherem, like Jericho; the by divine direction — the sentence God's command, not man's; the the Amorite's iniquity full — the judgment long deferred now ripe (Gen. 15:16).

The witnesses agree the utter destruction was no act of human cruelty but a divine judgment, the cherem ordered by God: the cities of the Amorite were devoted to destruction as Jericho would be, a sentence that could come only by God's express command; the severity, terrible to flesh, was the execution of the long-deferred judgment on the Amorite whose iniquity was now full (cf. Gen. 15:16), God using Israel as the instrument of his righteous wrath upon a people ripe for destruction.

So the witnesses make the verse utterly destroyed: The witnesses note the took all his cities: Heshbon and the rest. The witnesses note the utterly destroyed: devoted as cherem, like Jericho. The witnesses note the by divine direction: the sentence God's command, not man's. The witnesses note the the Amorite's iniquity full: the judgment long deferred now ripe (Gen. 15:16). The expositors observe the took all his cities: Heshbon and the rest. The expositors observe the utterly destroyed: devoted as cherem, like Jericho. The expositors observe the by divine direction: the sentence God's command, not man's. The expositors observe the the Amorite's iniquity full: the judgment long deferred now ripe (Gen. 15:16). The believer is taught to mark the took all his cities, the utterly destroyed, the by divine direction, the the Amorite's iniquity full, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel utterly destroyed the people of Sihon's cities — the cherem ordered by God, the long-deferred judgment on the Amorite whose iniquity was now full (Gen. 15:16).
35:
"Only the cattle we took for a prey unto ourselves, and the spoil of the cities which we took." The witnesses note the cattle and spoil of the cities taken by Israel for themselves. The witnesses note that Gill notes these they did not destroy but preserved alive for their own use and profit. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the cattle and the spoil taken as prey. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the only the cattle — spared from the destruction; the the spoil of the cities — taken as prey; the for themselves — the reward of the victory; the a measured judgment — the guilty destroyed, the goods given to Israel.

The witnesses agree the sparing of the cattle and goods, while the people were destroyed, marks the precise measure of the divine sentence: the judgment fell on the guilty inhabitants, but the spoil was given to Israel as the reward and provision of their God-given victory; the distinction shows that the cherem was a measured act of justice, not indiscriminate destruction, the lives forfeit to judgment and the goods granted to the conquerors — God both punishing the Amorite and providing for his people out of the same victory.

So the witnesses make the verse the spoil taken: The witnesses note the only the cattle: spared from the destruction. The witnesses note the the spoil of the cities: taken as prey. The witnesses note the for themselves: the reward of the victory. The witnesses note the a measured judgment: the guilty destroyed, the goods given to Israel. The expositors observe the only the cattle: spared from the destruction. The expositors observe the the spoil of the cities: taken as prey. The expositors observe the for themselves: the reward of the victory. The expositors observe the a measured judgment: the guilty destroyed, the goods given to Israel. The believer is taught to mark the only the cattle, the the spoil of the cities, the for themselves, the a measured judgment, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel took the cattle and spoil for themselves — the precise measure of the sentence, the guilty destroyed yet the goods given as provision, God punishing the Amorite and providing for his people in one victory.
36:
"From Aroer, which is by the brink of the river of Arnon, and from the city that is by the river, even unto Gilead, there was not one city too strong for us: the LORD our God delivered all unto us:" The witnesses note the whole land from Aroer to Gilead taken, not one city too strong, the LORD delivering all.
