The witnesses note that Gill notes Aroer was by the brink of Arnon, on the border of Moab, the principal city. The witnesses note that Clarke refers to the river of Arnon (Num. 21:13). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from Aroer to Gilead — the whole land taken; the not one city too strong — every stronghold falling; the the LORD delivered all — the conquest his doing; the no defence avails — against the God who fights for his people.

The witnesses agree the sweeping completeness of the conquest is ascribed to God's power: from Aroer to Gilead "there was not one city too strong," for "the LORD our God delivered all unto us"; the strength of walls and the height of fortifications availed nothing against the God who fought for Israel, every stronghold falling at his giving — a witness that no defence of man can stand when God has decreed a people's overthrow, and a ground of confidence for his people in every after conflict.

So the witnesses make the verse all delivered: The witnesses note the from Aroer to Gilead: the whole land taken. The witnesses note the not one city too strong: every stronghold falling. The witnesses note the the LORD delivered all: the conquest his doing. The witnesses note the no defence avails: against the God who fights for his people. The expositors observe the from Aroer to Gilead: the whole land taken. The expositors observe the not one city too strong: every stronghold falling. The expositors observe the the LORD delivered all: the conquest his doing. The expositors observe the no defence avails: against the God who fights for his people. The believer is taught to mark the from Aroer to Gilead, the not one city too strong, the the LORD delivered all, the no defence avails, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that the whole land from Aroer to Gilead fell, not one city too strong — the LORD delivering all, no defence of man standing against the God who fought for Israel.
37:
"Only unto the land of the children of Ammon thou camest not, nor unto any place of the river Jabbok, nor unto the cities in the mountains, nor unto whatsoever the LORD our God forbad us." The witnesses note that Israel touched not Ammon's land, nor any place the LORD had forbidden. The witnesses note that Gill notes the land of Ammon was then in their possession, and Israel came not unto any place of the river Jabbok, the border of Ammon, nor whatever God forbade. The witnesses note that they kept within the bounds the LORD had set. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the unto Ammon thou camest not — sparing their land; the nor the river Jabbok — the forbidden border; the whatsoever the LORD forbad — kept untouched; the obedience in victory — taking only what God gave.

The witnesses agree the chapter closes by marking Israel's exact obedience even in victory: flushed with conquest and able to take more, they yet touched not Ammon's land nor anything the LORD had forbidden, keeping precisely within the bounds God had set; the restraint amid triumph is the crown of the narrative — that Israel took only what God gave and spared what he reserved, their warfare governed not by appetite for gain but by obedience to his word, the victorious people as careful to obey as they were ready to fight.

So the witnesses make the verse the bounds kept: The witnesses note the unto Ammon thou camest not: sparing their land. The witnesses note the nor the river Jabbok: the forbidden border. The witnesses note the whatsoever the LORD forbad: kept untouched. The witnesses note the obedience in victory: taking only what God gave. The expositors observe the unto Ammon thou camest not: sparing their land. The expositors observe the nor the river Jabbok: the forbidden border. The expositors observe the whatsoever the LORD forbad: kept untouched. The expositors observe the obedience in victory: taking only what God gave. The believer is taught to mark the unto Ammon thou camest not, the nor the river Jabbok, the whatsoever the LORD forbad, the obedience in victory, and to trust the God who governs the nations. The verse thus records that Israel touched not Ammon's land nor anything God forbade — the crown of the narrative, obedience amid victory, taking only what God gave and sparing what he reserved.
